text: The surgeon repairs a symptomatic right inguinal hernia, revealing incarcerated omentum adherent to the inguinal canal.
The surgeon enters the preperitoneal plane, working from the level of the anterior superior iliac spine (ASIS) towards the medial umbilical ligaments, preparing for the hernia repair.
The surgeon performs medial dissection, adjusting to elevate the fat and retract the bladder downward.
The surgeon performs medial dissection followed by lateral dissection, focusing on the anatomical structures involved in the hernia repair.
The surgeon creates space for mesh placement by leaving some fat on the abdominal wall and identifying the cord structures, briefly inspecting the area.
The surgeon accesses the cord structures at the level of the anal ring, sweeping them downwards before moving back out of the inner-warf metal.

The surgeon clears the vas and cord structures, abandoning the tip of the sac without pursuing it into the inguinal canal. Fat is cleared over the femoral space to check for hernias, followed by dissection down to the obturator space.

The surgeon prioritizes the cord structures to create space for mesh placement, focusing on inferior and lateral areas. The video displays the four distinct hernia locations within the inguinal canal.

The surgeon observes the absence of a cord lipoma and proceeds to place the mesh over the inguinal canal. The mesh is positioned to cover the medial pubic area (MPO) effectively, and the surgeon uses sutures to close the flap.
The surgeon identifies a small hole in the hernia sac and uses a suture to close the flap, ensuring the sac remains closed.
